description: Two transactions acquiring the same rows/tables in opposite orders, producing a deadlock the engine resolves by aborting one.
body: Failure mode: transaction A locks row 1 then row 2 while B locks row 2 then row 1, so each waits on the other and the database kills one with a deadlock error. Detection signals: 'deadlock detected' errors under concurrency; intermittent transaction aborts that vanish at low load; the DB's deadlock log shows a lock cycle. Do not let concurrent transactions acquire resources in arbitrary orders. Safer fallback: acquire locks in a consistent global order (e.g. ascending primary key), keep transactions short, add a retry-on-deadlock loop for the loser, and verify under concurrent load that retries succeed.
